alt: Credential state ring from personalisation through enrolment, rotation and revocation, showing where hardware credentials keep keys on-chip across every transition.
desc: Credentials don't just get issued and used — they get re-issued, rotated, recovered, and revoked. The lifecycle every hardware-credential programme has to design.
alt_final: Credential state ring from personalisation through enrolment, rotation and revocation, showing where hardware credentials keep keys on-chip across every transition.

title: The Issuer Key Never Leaves the SAM
subtitle: Putting the issuer key in a Secure Access Module rather than validator firmware is the architectural difference between an incident at one validator and an incident across the whole fleet.
alt: Transit key hierarchy running from the issuer's HSM down to a per-tap session key, with K_app sealed inside the validator's Secure Access Module and never on the reader CPU.
h1: Why SAMs matter in closed-loop transit systems.
desc: Why every closed-loop transit deployment over a few thousand cards needs a Secure Access Module. Containment, diversification, auditable issuance, host channel.
alt_final: Transit key hierarchy running from the issuer's HSM down to a per-tap session key, with K_app sealed inside the validator's Secure Access Module and never on the reader CPU.

title: The Keys Cannot Live in Software
subtitle: In field-deployed OBUs and RSUs the attacker has physical access and unlimited time, so identity has to be rooted in a discrete tamper-resistant secure element rather than in firmware.
alt: Cutaway of a tamper-resistant secure element inside a V2X on-board unit, showing key custody that stays on-chip while the host MCU only passes commands.
desc: The secure element in connected vehicles: key isolation, hardware-bound identity, anti-cloning, secure boot, OTA trust anchors. SIM, MFF2, TPM vs HSM vs SE.
alt_final: Cutaway of a tamper-resistant secure element inside a V2X on-board unit, showing key custody that stays on-chip while the host MCU only passes commands.

title: EV2 Is the Architectural Pivot
subtitle: Across twelve years of DESFire the cipher never changed — what changed is how hard the platform makes it to use AES badly, which is why greenfield should buy EV3 silicon and personalise it in EV2 mode.
alt: Generation ladder across DESFire EV1, EV2 and EV3 — AES-128 stays constant while per-session secure messaging and transaction MAC change what a transit issuer can prove.
h1: DESFire EV1 vs EV2 vs EV3 — an architectural evolution.
desc: What actually changed across DESFire EV1, EV2, and EV3 — secure messaging, transaction MAC, key diversification, side-channel hardening. Architecture-level pillar post.
alt_final: Generation ladder across DESFire EV1, EV2 and EV3 — AES-128 stays constant while per-session secure messaging and transaction MAC change what a transit issuer can prove.

subtitle: An end-to-end FIDO2 implementation walkthrough — WebAuthn and CTAP2, the registration and authentication ceremonies, attestation, and resident-credential choices — with code that compiles.
alt: WebAuthn registration end to end: a server challenge, an origin-scoped keypair born inside the authenticator, attestation out, and only the public key stored server-side.
h1: Implementing FIDO2 Authentication: A Complete Developer Guide
desc: A practical, deployment-focused walkthrough of FIDO2 — CTAP2, WebAuthn, attestation, registration, authentication. Code that compiles, choices that scale.
alt_final: WebAuthn registration end to end: a server challenge, an origin-scoped keypair born inside the authenticator, attestation out, and only the public key stored server-side.

subtitle: WebAuthn is the easy part — what separates a weekend demo from a 10,000-seat rollout is the nine operational components around the protocol.

title: The Environment, Not the Algorithm
subtitle: Software-only device trust fails not because the cryptography is weak but because field deployment hands attackers physical access and unlimited time — a structural mismatch, not an implementation defect.
alt: A field device teardown showing why firmware-stored keys fall: exposed debug pads, an external SPI flash under a clip, and one extraction cloning a whole fleet.
desc: Why field-deployed hardware cannot rely on software identity: firmware extraction, key cloning, replay, fleet-scale compromise. Software vs TPM vs secure element.
alt_final: A field device teardown showing why firmware-stored keys fall: exposed debug pads, an external SPI flash under a clip, and one extraction cloning a whole fleet.

title: Signatures That Outlive the Certificate
subtitle: A signing platform is a stack — hardware-held credentials, an issuance CA, a trust list, a timestamp authority and an audit-signing key — and where the signing key lives decides everything else, including whether the signature is legally binding.
alt: A signature crossing its own certificate's expiry and still verifying, because LTV baked the OCSP response and an RFC 3161 timestamp into the CMS bundle at signing time.

desc: Architecture for S/MIME email signing, PDF/PKCS#7 document signing, hardware-backed credentials, Long-Term Validation, and the trust-list problem.

title: An ePassport Is Two Systems
subtitle: An ICAO 9303 ePassport programme is mostly the data-centre half — CSCA/DSC PKI, LDS generation, SOd signing, personalisation and inspection support — and that is where the seams break.
alt: The issuing half of an ICAO 9303 passport programme: CSCA signs short-lived DSCs, a DSC signs each SOd, and the PKD publishes the chain for border verifiers.

alt_final: The issuing half of an ICAO 9303 passport programme: CSCA signs short-lived DSCs, a DSC signs each SOd, and the PKD publishes the chain for border verifiers.

subtitle: The threat list is endless but the list that should drive identity and hardware-credential spending is short — and most of it is answered by origin-bound credentials and hardware-rooted identity.

subtitle: Hardware-backed IoT identity is not one applet but five — provisioning, attestation, mTLS, signed firmware and key rotation — running on a CC EAL6+ secure element the host MCU can never read.

title: Decoded Attestation Is Decorative Attestation
subtitle: Attestation is the policy primitive that lets an enterprise require certified hardware — but only if the relying party verifies the signature and validates the x5c chain against an MDS-derived trust anchor, rather than merely decoding it.

title: One Secure Element, One Revocation Surface
subtitle: The same CC EAL6+ secure element, packaged as a removable 4FF nano-card or a solderable MFF2 module, can carry the enterprise FIDO2 credential under the issuer's own keys — collapsing two certification and personalisation pipelines into one.

alt_final: One CC EAL6+ secure element in two packages — removable 4FF nano-card and solderable MFF2 — running a FIDO2 applet whose P-256 private key never leaves the chip boundary.

title: Twelve Billion Credentials Already Leaked
subtitle: Attackers already hold billions of leaked credentials, so the value of a second factor — and especially a hardware-bound one — is that it cuts off the long tail of credential stuffing and phishing replay.
alt: Credential-stuffing funnel showing why a password gate falls open, an OTP gate only raises attacker cost, and an origin-bound hardware factor blocks credential reuse outright.
desc: Cybercriminals have billions of leaked credentials. A second factor — especially a hardware-bound one — cuts off the long tail of credential-stuffing and phishing.

subtitle: The EU Cyber Resilience Act makes cybersecurity a whole-life product requirement for anything connected, and the hardware decisions behind it have to be made well before the 2027 deadline.

title: Two Authorities Keep the Vehicle Anonymous
subtitle: V2X PKI diverges from web PKI by design: two issuing authorities split identity from authorisation so a signed safety message proves certified hardware without revealing which vehicle sent it.
alt: The V2X trust fork: an Enrolment Authority and an Authorisation Authority split identity from authorisation so an OBU's signed CAM proves hardware, not identity.

alt_final: The V2X trust fork: an Enrolment Authority and an Authorisation Authority split identity from authorisation so an OBU's signed CAM proves hardware, not identity.

title: MFA Was a Strategy, Passkeys Are a Primitive
subtitle: Passkeys are not a stronger factor bolted onto MFA — they are a different cryptographic primitive whose origin binding retires phishing, replay, credential stuffing and push fatigue as attack classes.
alt: Why an adversary-in-the-middle proxy replays a one-time code but cannot use a passkey: the assertion is bound to the relying-party origin it never matches.
desc: Passkeys are not MFA-just-stronger. They are a different cryptographic primitive that retires phishing, replay, credential stuffing, and push fatigue as attack classes.
alt_final: Why an adversary-in-the-middle proxy replays a one-time code but cannot use a passkey: the assertion is bound to the relying-party origin it never matches.

title: Pick on Lifecycle, Not Security
subtitle: PIV cards, USB tokens and embedded secure elements are all hardware-backed and all equally secure — the workforce decision turns on lifecycle, physical-logical convergence, and what happens at recovery time.
alt: PIV card, USB token and soldered secure element compared on lifecycle — issuance CA, help-desk setup, or MDM channel — since all three seal the workforce private key equally.

title: One Million Devices, One Million Keys
subtitle: A million non-clonable device identities demand HSM-derived per-device keys, SCP03 custody on the personalisation line, OTA rotation without recall, and revocation tuned to each connectivity tier.
alt: Factory-to-field identity: HSM-derived per-device keys land inside secure elements over SCP03, leave an append-only audit trail, then rotate over the air.

title: Cost Per Extraction Decides the Architecture
subtitle: Hardware does not make key theft impossible — it moves cost-per-extraction by orders of magnitude, and that economics is what decides whether authentication holds at fleet scale.

desc: Hardware moves the cost-per-extraction of a credential up by orders of magnitude. The threat surfaces software cannot defend, and what hardware changes.
alt_final: A logarithmic ladder of what stealing one credential costs an attacker, from a $5 phishing kit to millions against a CC EAL6+ secure element boundary.

title: Can an Attacker Extract Your Keys
subtitle: Secure by design, for connected hardware, reduces to one question a patch cannot answer later — can an attacker holding the device extract its keys — and that is settled by the trust layer in silicon.
alt: AmbiSEC as the trust layer under a connected node: keys and the update verifier sit below the firmware line, so application code can never read what it protects.

title: The CA Should Never Sign Blind
subtitle: The procedural side of PKI — CA/RA/VA roles, offline-root hierarchy, HSM key custody and attestation in the issuance flow — is what decides whether a workforce or government credential programme holds up.

subtitle: FIDO2 / WebAuthn replaces the password with an origin-bound public-key signature generated inside a hardware authenticator, so a look-alike domain can never obtain a usable credential.
